summary: Julia Evans shares her personal experience of accepting a job offer with stock options she didn't understand, and walks through the math that reveals how expensive and risky exercising options can be. She explains that when you leave a private company, you typically have only 3 months to exercise your options, which can cost hundreds of thousands of dollars in exercise prices plus taxes on unrealized gains. Using both a hypothetical example and her own situation at Stripe, she shows how the total cost of exercising can exceed an employee's entire savings. The post provides a concrete list of basic and advanced questions to ask before accepting an offer, covering everything from exercise prices to liquidation preferences and early exercise options.
key_points: 65% of people surveyed accepted job offers without understanding how their stock options work, leaving significant money decisions uninformed
When you leave a private company, you typically have only 3 months to exercise your ISOs, and the combined cost of exercise price plus taxes on the spread can be unaffordable
Tax agencies may treat the difference between exercise price and fair market value as income you owe taxes on, even though you can't actually sell the stock yet
VCs often have liquidation preferences guaranteeing they get their money back first, which can make employee shares worthless even in an acquisition
The company making you an offer has lawyers who've thought carefully about all of this — you should be at least somewhat informed too
money_quotes: Stock options are BORING AND COMPLICATED AND AWFUL. They are full of taxes, which we all know are awful.
Surprise! Now you need to pay hundreds of thousands of dollars to invest in an uncertain outcome.
Your local tax agency thinks you have made 3.3 million dollars. Well, you haven't actually made that, since you're buying stock you can't sell.
The total amount it would cost to exercise + pay taxes on my options is more than all of the money I have.
By definition it is impossible for me to exercise my options without borrowing money.
Your employer has all the power.
Being informed isn't disloyal. The company you work for is informed.
The company making you an offer has lawyers and they should know the answers to all the questions I suggested. They've thought very carefully about these things already.
key_insight: Stock options at private companies can be effectively worthless to employees who can't afford the exercise price and tax bill when they leave, so you should do the math before accepting an offer rather than after.
